**Second lieutenant** is a junior [commissioned officer](/wiki/Officer_(armed_forces) "Officer (armed forces)") [military rank](/wiki/Military_rank "Military rank") in many [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_forces "Armed forces").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The lowest officer rank, it is usually placed below [lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant") or [first lieutenant](/wiki/First_lieutenant "First lieutenant").

## Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_lieutenant&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Australia")]

The rank of second lieutenant existed in the [military forces of the Australian colonies](/wiki/Colonial_forces_of_Australia "Colonial forces of Australia") and [Australian Army](/wiki/Australian_Army "Australian Army") until 1986.

In the colonial forces, which closely followed the practices of the British military, the rank of second lieutenant began to replace ranks such as [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(rank) "Ensign (rank)") and [cornet](/wiki/Cornet_(military_rank) "Cornet (military rank)") from 1871.

New appointments to the rank of second lieutenant ceased in the regular army in 1986.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Immediately prior to this change, the rank had been effectively reserved for new graduates from the [Officer Cadet School, Portsea](/wiki/Officer_Cadet_School,_Portsea "Officer Cadet School, Portsea") which closed in 1985. (Graduates of the [Australian Defence Force Academy](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force_Academy "Australian Defence Force Academy") (ADFA) and the [Royal Military College, Duntroon](/wiki/Royal_Military_College,_Duntroon "Royal Military College, Duntroon") (RMC-D) are commissioned as [lieutenants](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant").).[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The rank of second lieutenant is only appointed to officers in special appointments such as training institutions, university regiments and while under probation during training. Trainees undertaking Special Service Officer (SSO) training are appointed at their officer rank prior to undertaking the training (usually as lieutenants or captains). This is different to General Service Officer (GSO) trainees who start off at the rank of officer cadet (ADFA/[Australian Army Reserve](/wiki/Australian_Army_Reserve "Australian Army Reserve") officer trainees) or staff cadet ([Royal Military College, Duntroon](/wiki/Royal_Military_College,_Duntroon "Royal Military College, Duntroon")).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Ranks equivalent to second lieutenant are acting sub-lieutenant in the [Royal Australian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy "Royal Australian Navy") and [pilot officer](/wiki/Pilot_officer "Pilot officer") in the [Royal Australian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Air_Force "Royal Australian Air Force").

## Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_lieutenant&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Canada")]

The [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") adopted the rank with insignia of a single gold ring around the service dress uniform cuff for both army and air personnel upon unification in 1968 until the late 2000s.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) For a time, naval personnel used this rank but reverted to the Royal Canadian Navy rank of [acting sub-lieutenant](/wiki/Acting_Sub-Lieutenant "Acting Sub-Lieutenant"), though the CF green uniform was retained until the mid-1980s. Currently, the [Canadian Army](/wiki/Canadian_Army "Canadian Army") insignia for second lieutenant is a *pip* and the [Royal Canadian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force "Royal Canadian Air Force") insignia for lieutenant is one thick braid. The equivalent rank for the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") is [acting sub-lieutenant](/wiki/Acting_sub-lieutenant "Acting sub-lieutenant"). Also known as an Ensign in the Foot Guards units ([Canadian Grenadier Guards](/wiki/Canadian_Grenadier_Guards "Canadian Grenadier Guards") & [Governor General's Foot Guards](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Foot_Guards "Governor General's Foot Guards")).

## France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_lieutenant&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: France")]

*Sous-lieutenant* is the most junior commissioned rank in the modern French Army, Airforce and *[Gendarmerie](/wiki/Gendarmerie "Gendarmerie")*. It dates historically from the late XVIth Century in the reign of [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France"). In 1674 this designation replaced that of ensign in the French infantry. By 1791 there were two *sous-lieutenants* in each company. After 1781 entry to this rank and beyond required four quartering of nobility - that is for all four grandparents to be recognised members of the aristocracy. The French Revolution substituted election by popular vote and later, under Napoleon, by graduation from officers' military schools or after four years of service as a *sous-officer* (NCO).

Until World War I *sous-lieutenants* were distinguished by a single gold fringed [epaulette](/wiki/Epaulette "Epaulette") worn on the right shoulder (silver for cavalry).[[8]](#cite_note-8) Modern rank insignia is reduced to a single braid stripe on each shoulder strap and (when worn) [kepi](/wiki/Kepi "Kepi").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Indonesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_lieutenant&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Indonesia")]

Main article: [Indonesian military ranks](/wiki/Indonesian_military_ranks "Indonesian military ranks")

In [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), "second lieutenant" is known as *letnan dua* (*letda*) which is the most junior ranked officer in the [Indonesian Military](/wiki/Indonesian_Military "Indonesian Military"). Officers in the Indonesian National Armed Forces are commissioned through one of four major commissioning programs. Upon graduation the candidates are promoted to the rank of second lieutenant, thus becoming [commissioned officers](/wiki/Commissioned_officer "Commissioned officer"). The four programs are:

* National Armed Forces Academy (*Akademi TNI*): a four-year undergraduate program that emphasizes instruction in the arts, sciences, and professions, preparing men and women to take on the challenge of being officers in the armed forces (Army: [Military Academy](/wiki/Indonesian_Military_Academy "Indonesian Military Academy"), Navy: [Naval Academy](/wiki/Indonesian_Naval_Academy "Indonesian Naval Academy"), Air Force: [Air Force Academy](/wiki/Indonesian_Air_Force_Academy "Indonesian Air Force Academy"));
* [Officer Candidate School](/wiki/Officer_Candidate_School_(Indonesian_Army) "Officer Candidate School (Indonesian Army)"): a 28-week program that is attended by senior [NCOs](/wiki/Non-commissioned_officer "Non-commissioned officer") or [warrant officers](/wiki/Warrant_officer "Warrant officer") from all services;
* Career Officer Program for college graduates: a 7–8 month program that is designed to recruit civilian professionals (e.g., doctors, dentists, pharmacists, psychologists) into the armed forces;
* Pilot Short Service School: a 34-month program to train pilots to serve in the armed forces.

## New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_lieutenant&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Like many other Commonwealth countries, the rank structures of the [New Zealand Defence Force](/wiki/New_Zealand_Defence_Force "New Zealand Defence Force") usually follow British traditions. Hence the [New Zealand Army](/wiki/New_Zealand_Army "New Zealand Army") maintains a rank of second lieutenant and the [Royal New Zealand Air Force](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Air_Force "Royal New Zealand Air Force") has its exact equivalent, [pilot officer](/wiki/Pilot_officer "Pilot officer").

However, the [Royal New Zealand Navy](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Navy "Royal New Zealand Navy") breaks with British tradition and uses the name [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(rank) "Ensign (rank)") for its most junior commissioned officer rank (rather than the usual equivalents, such as acting sub-lieutenant or second lieutenant).

## United Kingdom and other Commonwealth countries (excepting Australia, Bangladesh, and Canada)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_lieutenant&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: United Kingdom and other Commonwealth countries (excepting Australia, Bangladesh, and Canada)")]

The rank of second lieutenant (2Lt) was introduced throughout the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") in 1877 to replace the short-lived rank of [sub-lieutenant](/wiki/Sub-lieutenant#Army_and_air_force_rank "Sub-lieutenant"), although it had long been used in the [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery"), [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers"), [Fusilier](/wiki/Fusilier "Fusilier") and [Rifle](/wiki/The_Rifle_Brigade "The Rifle Brigade") [regiments](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment"). At first the rank bore no distinct insignia. The rank was abolished in 1881 but reintroduced in 1887.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1902, a single [Bath star](/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath#Habit_and_insignia "Order of the Bath") was introduced; the ranks of [lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant_(British_Army_and_Royal_Marines) "Lieutenant (British Army and Royal Marines)") and [captain](/wiki/Captain_(British_Army_and_Royal_Marines) "Captain (British Army and Royal Marines)") had their number of stars increased by one to (respectively) two and three. The rank is also used by the [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines").

New British Army officers are normally commissioned as second lieutenants at the end of their commissioning course at [RMA Sandhurst](/wiki/RMA_Sandhurst "RMA Sandhurst") where they hold the rank of [Officer Cadet](/wiki/Officer_cadet_(British_Armed_Forces) "Officer cadet (British Armed Forces)"), and continue with specific training with their units. Progression to lieutenant rank usually occurs after about a year. In the British armed forces, *second lieutenant* is a rank which is not used as a form of address. Instead a second lieutenant named, for example, *Smith* is addressed and referred to as *Mr Smith*, with the exception that the alternative titles *ensign* ([Foot Guards](/wiki/Foot_Guards "Foot Guards")) and *cornet* (in the [Blues and Royals](/wiki/Blues_and_Royals "Blues and Royals")[[11]](#cite_note-11) and [Queen's Royal Hussars](/wiki/Queen%27s_Royal_Hussars "Queen's Royal Hussars")[[12]](#cite_note-12)) are still used.
In the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), the comparable rank is [pilot officer](/wiki/Pilot_officer "Pilot officer"). The equivalent in the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") is [Midshipman](/wiki/Midshipman#Naval_rank "Midshipman").

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), second lieutenant is the normal entry-level rank for most [commissioned officers](/wiki/Officer_(armed_forces) "Officer (armed forces)") in the [Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"), [Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps"), [Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"), and [Space Force](/wiki/United_States_Space_Force "United States Space Force"). It is equivalent to the rank of [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(rank) "Ensign (rank)") in the [Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"), [Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard"), [Public Health Service Commissioned Corps](/wiki/United_States_Public_Health_Service_Commissioned_Corps "United States Public Health Service Commissioned Corps"), and [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Commissioned Officer Corps](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration_Commissioned_Officer_Corps "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Commissioned Officer Corps").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the Army and Marine Corps, a second lieutenant typically is a [platoon leader](/wiki/Platoon_leader "Platoon leader") who leads a [platoon](/wiki/Platoon "Platoon")-size element, usually consisting of 16 to 44 soldiers or marines.[[14]](#cite_note-14) A rifle platoon is composed of several [squads](/wiki/Squad "Squad") with each squad led by a non-commissioned officer as [squad leader](/wiki/Squad_leader "Squad leader"). The second lieutenant is usually assisted by a [platoon sergeant](/wiki/Platoon_sergeant "Platoon sergeant") who advises and supports the platoon's commanding officer in leading the unit.

In the Army, the rank bore no [insignia](/wiki/United_States_Army_officer_rank_insignia "United States Army officer rank insignia") until December 1917, when a gold-colored bar similar to the silver-colored bar of a [first lieutenant](/wiki/First_lieutenant#U.S._Army,_U.S._Marine_Corps,_U.S._Air_Force,_and_U.S._Space_Force "First lieutenant") was introduced.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In U.S. [military slang](/wiki/Military_slang "Military slang"), the rank is sometimes called "butterbar" or "brown bar" in reference to the insignia,[[16]](#cite_note-16) while "shavetail"—a reference to an [unbroken](/wiki/Horse_training "Horse training") mule—is a derisive nickname.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_lieutenant&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

The rank of second lieutenant has existed in the [Bangladesh Army](/wiki/Bangladesh_Army "Bangladesh Army") since the [Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War"). It is a rank below [lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant") and a rank above [master warrant officer](/wiki/Master_warrant_officer "Master warrant officer"). In the Army, a second lieutenant serves as the administrative officer or staff officer in a unit.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In the Navy, the rank of second lieutenant does not exist, its equivalent is [sub-lieutenant](/wiki/Sub-lieutenant "Sub-lieutenant").[[19]](#cite_note-19)
